cat > pyproject.toml << 'EOF'
[project]
name = "research-workspace"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Research workspace for detecting different types of lies in LLMs"
requires-python = ">=3.10"
dependencies = []

[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"
EOF
uv venv && source .venv/bin/activate && uv add pypdf requests arxiv datasets && mkdir -p papers datasets code

uthors": "Hanyu Duan, Yi Yang, K. Tam",
      "url": "https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusId:267682191",
      "relevance": 2,
      "abstract": "Large Language Models (LLMs) can make up answers that are not real, and this is known as hallucination. This research aims to see if, how, and to what extent LLMs are aware of hallucination. More specifically, we check whether and how an LLM reacts differently in its hidden states when it answers a question right versus when it hallucinates. To do this, we introduce an experimental framework which allows examining LLM's hidden states in different hallucination situations. Building upon this framework, we conduct a series of experiments with language models in the LLaMA family (Touvron et al., 2023). Our empirical findings suggest that LLMs react differently when processing a genuine response versus a fabricated one. We then apply various model interpretation techniques to help understand and explain the findings better. Moreover, informed by the empirical observations, we show great potential of using the guidance derived from LLM's hidden representation space to mitigate hallucination. We believe this work provides insights into how LLMs produce hallucinated answers and how to make them occur less often.",
      "citations": 55
    },
    {
      "title": "TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods",
      "year": 2021,
      "authors": "Stephanie C. Lin, Jacob Hilton, Owain Evans",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/77d956cdab4508d569ae5741549b78e715fd0749",
      "relevance": 2,
      "abstract": "We propose a benchmark to measure whether a language model is truthful in generating answers to questions. The benchmark comprises 817 questions that span 38 categories, including health, law, finance and politics. We crafted questions that some humans would answer falsely due to a false belief or misconception. To perform well, models must avoid generating false answers learned from imitating human texts. We tested GPT-3, GPT-Neo/J, GPT-2 and a T5-based model. The best model was truthful on 58% of questions, while human performance was 94%. Models generated many false answers that mimic popular misconceptions and have the potential to deceive humans. The largest models were generally the least truthful. This contrasts with other NLP tasks, where performance improves with model size. However, this result is expected if false answers are learned from the training distribution. We suggest that scaling up models alone is less promising for improving truthfulness than fine-tuning using training objectives other than imitation of text from the web.",
      "citations": 2977
    },
    {
      "title": "AI-Generated Content in Cross-Domain Applications: Research Trends, Challenges and Propositions",
      "year": 2025,
      "authors": "Jianxin Li, Liang Qu, Taotao Cai, Zhixue Zhao, Nur Al Hasan Haldar, Aneesh Krishna, Xiangjie Kong, F. Macau, Tanmoy Chakraborty, Aniket Deroy, Binshan Lin, Karen Blackmore, N. Noman, Jingxian Cheng, Ningning Cui, Jianliang Xu",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/77b79841c100b854d9789d820d1187e2aced574c",
      "relevance": 2,
      "abstract": "Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) has rapidly emerged with the capability to generate different forms of content, including text, images, videos, and other modalities, which can achieve a quality similar to content created by humans. As a result, AIGC is now widely applied across various domains such as digital marketing, education, and public health, and has shown promising results by enhancing content creation efficiency and improving information delivery. However, there are few studies that explore the latest progress and emerging challenges of AIGC across different domains. To bridge this gap, this paper brings together 16 scholars from multiple disciplines to provide a cross-domain perspective on the trends and challenges of AIGC. Specifically, the contributions of this paper are threefold: (1) It first provides a broader overview of AIGC, spanning the training techniques of Generative AI, detection methods, and both the spread and use of AI-generated content across digital platforms. (2) It then introduces the societal impacts of AIGC across diverse domains, along with a review of existing methods employed in these contexts. (3) Finally, it discusses the key technical challenges and presents research propositions to guide future work. Through these contributions, this vision paper seeks to offer readers a cross-domain perspective on AIGC, providing insights into its current research trends, ongoing challenges, and future directions.",
      "citations": 4
    },
    {
      "title": "A Survey on Hallucination in Large Language Models: Principles, Taxonomy, Challenges, and Open Questions",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "Lei Huang, Weijiang Yu, Weitao Ma, Weihong Zhong, Zhangyin Feng, Haotian Wang, Qianglong Chen, Weihua Peng, Xiaocheng Feng, Bing Qin, Ting Liu",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/1e909e2a8cdacdcdff125ebcc566f37cb869a1c8",
      "relevance": 2,
      "abstract": "The emergence of large language models (LLMs) has marked a significant breakthrough in natural language processing (NLP), fueling a paradigm shift in information acquisition. Nevertheless, LLMs are prone to hallucination, generating plausible yet nonfactual content. This phenomenon raises significant concerns over the reliability of LLMs in real-world information retrieval (IR) systems and has attracted intensive research to detect and mitigate such hallucinations. Given the open-ended general-purpose attributes inherent to LLMs, LLM hallucinations present distinct challenges that diverge from prior task-specific models. This divergence highlights the urgency for a nuanced understanding and comprehensive overview of recent advances in LLM hallucinations. In this survey, we begin with an innovative taxonomy of hallucination in the era of LLM and then delve into the factors contributing to hallucinations. Subsequently, we present a thorough overview of hallucination detection methods and benchmarks. Our discussion then transfers to representative methodologies for mitigating LLM hallucinations. Additionally, we delve into the current limitations faced by retrieval-augmented LLMs in combating hallucinations, offering insights for developing more robust IR systems. Finally, we highlight the promising research directions on LLM hallucinations, including hallucination in large vision-language models and understanding of knowledge boundaries in LLM hallucinations.",
      "citations": 2357
    },
    {
      "title": "The Internal State of an LLM Knows When its Lying",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "A. Azaria, Tom M. Mitchell",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/f406aceba4f29cc7cfbe7edb2f52f01374486589",
      "relevance": 2,
      "abstract": "While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown exceptional performance in various tasks, one of their most prominent drawbacks is generating inaccurate or false information with a confident tone. In this paper, we provide evidence that the LLM's internal state can be used to reveal the truthfulness of statements. This includes both statements provided to the LLM, and statements that the LLM itself generates. Our approach is to train a classifier that outputs the probability that a statement is truthful, based on the hidden layer activations of the LLM as it reads or generates the statement. Experiments demonstrate that given a set of test sentences, of which half are true and half false, our trained classifier achieves an average of 71\\% to 83\\% accuracy labeling which sentences are true versus false, depending on the LLM base model. Furthermore, we explore the relationship between our classifier's performance and approaches based on the probability assigned to the sentence by the LLM. We show that while LLM-assigned sentence probability is related to sentence truthfulness, this probability is also dependent on sentence length and the frequencies of words in the sentence, resulting in our trained classifier providing a more reliable approach to detecting truthfulness, highlighting its potential to enhance the reliability of LLM-generated content and its practical applicability in real-world scenarios.",
      "citations": 555
    },
    {
      "title": "Chain-of-Verification Reduces Hallucination in Large Language Models",
      "year": 2023,
      "authors": "S. Dhuliawala, M. Komeili, Jing Xu, R. Raileanu, Xian Li, Asli Celikyilmaz, Jason Weston",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/4b0b56be0ae9479d2bd5c2f0943db1906343c10f",
      "relevance": 2,
      "abstract": "Generation of plausible yet incorrect factual information, termed hallucination, is an unsolved issue in large language models. We study the ability of language models to deliberate on the responses they give in order to correct their mistakes. We develop the Chain-of-Verification (CoVe) method whereby the model first (i) drafts an initial response; then (ii) plans verification questions to fact-check its draft; (iii) answers those questions independently so the answers are not biased by other responses; and (iv) generates its final verified response. In experiments, we show CoVe decreases hallucinations across a variety of tasks, from list-based questions from Wikidata, closed book MultiSpanQA and longform text generation.",
      "citations": 383
    },
    {
      "title": "A Survey on the Use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in Fake News",
      "year": 2024,
      "authors": "Eleftheria Papageorgiou, Christos Chronis, Iraklis Varlamis, Yassine Himeur",
      "url": "https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/127811f1f415af7c5d4ade28f118801f0548f694",
      "relevance": 2,
      "abstract": "The proliferation of fake news and fake profiles on social media platforms poses significant threats to information integrity and societal trust. Traditional detection methods, including rule-based approaches, metadata analysis, and human fact-checking, have been employed to combat disinformation, but these methods often fall short in the face of increasingly sophisticated fake content. This review article explores the emerging role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in enhancing the detection of fake news and fake profiles. We provide a comprehensive overview of the nature and spread of disinformation, followed by an examination of existing detection methodologies. The article delves into the capabilities of LLMs in generating both fake news and fake profiles, highlighting their dual role as both a tool for disinformation and a powerful means of detection. We discuss the various applications of LLMs in text classification, fact-checking, verification, and contextual analysis, demonstrating how these models surpass traditional methods in accuracy and efficiency. Additionally, the article covers LLM-based detection of fake profiles through profile attribute analysis, network analysis, and behavior pattern recognition. Through comparative analysis, we showcase the advantages of LLMs over conventional techniques and present case studies that illustrate practical applications. Despite their potential, LLMs face challenges such as computational demands and ethical concerns, which we discuss in more detail. The review concludes with future directions for research and development in LLM-based fake news and fake profile detection, underscoring the importance of continued innovation to safeguard the authenticity of online information.",
      "citations": 51
    },
    {
      "title": "Toward Mitigating Misinformation and Social Media Manipulation in LLM Era",
      "year": 2024,
      "authors": "Yizhou Zhang, Karishma Sharma, Lun Du, Yan Liu",

      "abstract": "Large language models (LLMs) can\"lie\", which we define as outputting false statements despite\"knowing\"the truth in a demonstrable sense. LLMs might\"lie\", for example, when instructed to output misinformation. Here, we develop a simple lie detector that requires neither access to the LLM's activations (black-box) nor ground-truth knowledge of the fact in question. The detector works by asking a predefined set of unrelated follow-up questions after a suspected lie, and feeding the LLM's yes/no answers into a logistic regression classifier. Despite its simplicity, this lie detector is highly accurate and surprisingly general. When trained on examples from a single setting -- prompting GPT-3.5 to lie about factual questions -- the detector generalises out-of-distribution to (1) other LLM architectures, (2) LLMs fine-tuned to lie, (3) sycophantic lies, and (4) lies emerging in real-life scenarios such as sales.       "abstract": "Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionised natural language processing, exhibiting impressive human-like capabilities. However, other researchers showed that these classifiers may fail to generalise, for example to negated statements. In this work, we aim to develop a robust method to detect when an LLM is lying. To this end, we make the following key contributions: (i) We demonstrate the existence of a two-dimensional subspace, along which the activation vectors of true and false statements can be separated. Notably, this finding is universal and holds for various LLMs, including Gemma-7B, LLaMA2-13B, Mistral-7B and LLaMA3-8B.       "abstract": "Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities across a variety of tasks, but their increasing autonomy in real-world applications raises concerns about their trustworthiness.       "relevance": 3,
      "abstract": "Deception is a fundamental challenge for multi-agent reasoning: effective systems must strategically conceal information while detecting misleading behavior in others.       "abstract": "Large language model (LLM) systems, such as ChatGPT1 or Gemini2, can show impressive reasoning and question-answering capabilities but often \u2018hallucinate\u2019 false outputs and unsubstantiated answers3,4. content.text:
Good, I have results from two searches. Let me now identify the most relevant papers and start downloading them while the third search completes.

The key papers I've identified so far:

2. "Detecting hallucinations using semantic entropy" - Farquhar et al. 2024 (Nature)
3. "TruthfulQA" - Lin et al. 2021
4. "Deception abilities emerged in LLMs" - Hagendorff 2023
5. "A Survey on Hallucination in LLMs" - Huang et al. 2023

7. "HAD: Hallucination Detection" - Xu et al. 2025

source .venv/bin/activate && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import requests, os, time

papers = [
    # (filename, url)
    ("2304.13734_internal_state_lying.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2304.13734.pdf"),
    ("2302.09611_semantic_entropy.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2302.09611.pdf"),
    ("2109.07958_truthfulqa.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2109.07958.pdf"),
    ("2307.16513_deception_abilities_llm.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.16513.pdf"),
    ("2311.05232_hallucination_survey.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2311.05232.pdf"),
    ("2407.01072_hack_hallucination_axes.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2407.01072.pdf"),
    ("2502.04427_had_hallucination_detection.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.04427.pdf"),
    ("2408.02431_llm_lie_detection_diplomacy.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2408.02431.pdf"),
    ("2406.04175_confabulation_value.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.04175.pdf"),
    ("2309.13788_llm_misinformation_detected.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.13788.pdf"),
    ("2402.02181_llm_know_hallucinate.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.02181.pdf"),
    ("2406.09569_mind_hallucination.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2406.09569.pdf"),
    ("2403.04307_halueval_wild.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2403.04307.pdf"),
    ("2310.18344_chain_of_verification.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.18344.pdf"),
]

os.makedirs("papers", exist_ok=True)
for fname, url in papers:
    path = f"papers/{fname}"
    if os.path.exists(path):
        print(f"SKIP {fname}")
        continue
    try:
        r = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        if r.status_code == 200 and len(r.content) > 10000:
            with open(path, 'wb') as f:
                f.write(r.content)
            print(f"OK   {fname} ({len(r.content)//1024}KB)")
        else:
            print(f"FAIL {fname} status={r.status_code} size={len(r.content)}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"ERR  {fname}: {e}")
    time.sleep(0.5)
PYEOF

Based on the search results, here's information about the FEVER fact verification dataset:

## Overview

FEVER (Fact Extraction and VERification) consists of 185,445 claims generated by altering sentences extracted from Wikipedia and subsequently verified without knowledge of the sentence they were derived from.

## Dataset Structure

The claims are classified as Supported, Refuted or NotEnoughInfo. For the first two classes, the annotators also recorded the sentence(s) forming the necessary evidence for their judgment.

## Publication

The dataset was created by James Thorne, Andreas Vlachos, Christos Christodoulopoulos, and Arpit Mittal and published in 2018 in the Proceedings of the 2018 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies (NAACL-HLT).

## Task and Challenge

The task is verification of textual claims against textual sources. When compared to textual entailment (TE)/natural language inference, the key difference is that in these tasks the passage to verify each claim is given, and in recent years it typically consists of a single sentence, while in verification systems it is retrieved from a large set of documents in order to form the evidence.

## Extended Version

There's also an extended version: FEVER 2.0 Adversarial Attacks Dataset consists of 1174 claims created by the submissions of participants in the Breaker phase of the 2019 shared task. Participants were tasked with generating adversarial examples that induce classification errors for the existing systems.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about HaluEval:

## Overview

HaluEval is a large collection of generated and human-annotated hallucinated samples for evaluating the performance of LLMs in recognizing hallucination.

## Dataset Composition

HaluEval includes 5,000 general user queries with ChatGPT responses and 30,000 task-specific examples from three tasks: question answering, knowledge-grounded dialogue, and text summarization. The directory data contains 35K generated and human-annotated hallucinated samples used in the experiments.

## Methodology

To generate these samples, a ChatGPT-based two-step framework was proposed, i.e., sampling-then-filtering.

## Key Findings

The empirical results suggest that ChatGPT is likely to generate hallucinated content in specific topics by fabricating unverifiable information (i.e., about 19.5% responses). Providing external knowledge or adding reasoning steps can help LLMs recognize hallucinations.

## Hugging Face Availability

The HaluEval dataset is available on Hugging Face. The dataset was originally published in the paper HaluEval: A Large-Scale Hallucination Evaluation Benchmark for Large Language Models. You can find it at the `flowaicom/HaluEval` repository on Hugging Face Datasets.

The original benchmark is also maintained at https://github.com/RUCAIBox/HaluEval.

Based on the search results, I've found comprehensive information about TruthfulQA as a hallucination detection and LLM benchmark. Here's what the research shows:

## TruthfulQA Overview

TruthfulQA is a frequently used benchmark for evaluating the factuality, hallucination, and reliability of language models, having provided the insight that language models can internalize human falsehoods present in their training data.

## Dataset Characteristics

TruthfulQA consists of 817–790 open-ended and multiple-choice questions spanning 38 knowledge domains (health, finance, history, myth, etc.), with each item designed to provoke common misconceptions or untruthful responses, with labels distinguishing "best," "good," and "bad" answers, and additional adversarial design ensuring that mere fluency is insufficient for high performance.

## Role in Hallucination Benchmarking

The Hallucination Leaderboard includes tasks for Closed-book Open-domain QA with TruthfulQA (MC1, MC2, and Generative). TruthfulQA is designed to rigorously assess the capacity of models to resist generating human-plausible falsehoods and replicate only verified facts, especially in adversarial situations.

## Important Caveats

However, recent research has identified concerns with TruthfulQA. Analysis reveals that TruthfulQA is now saturated due to inclusion in training data, contains incorrect gold answers, and its metrics excessively penalize models. TruthfulQA is often misunderstood as a hallucination benchmark when it measures factuality.

This distinction is important because different mitigation strategies are required for hallucination and LLM factuality, and progress should be tracked by dedicated benchmarks.

I found several relevant resources on sycophancy detection in LLMs. Here are the key findings:

## Major Benchmarks and Datasets

**SycEval**: A framework to evaluate sycophantic behavior in ChatGPT-4o, Claude-Sonnet, and Gemini-1.5-Pro across AMPS (mathematics) and MedQuad (medical advice) datasets. Sycophantic behavior was observed in 58.19% of cases, with Gemini exhibiting the highest rate (62.47%) and ChatGPT the lowest (56.71%).

**ELEPHANT**: A benchmark for measuring social sycophancy, characterizing sycophancy as excessive preservation of a user's face (their desired self-image). Applying the benchmark to 11 models, LLMs consistently exhibit high rates of social sycophancy: on average, they preserve user's face 45 percentage points more than humans in general advice queries and in queries describing clear user wrongdoing (from Reddit's r/AmITheAsshole).

**Syco-bench**: A four-part benchmark to evaluate how much models flatter and defer to their users, consisting of tests for: Picking Sides (how much the model sides with the user over a friend in an argument), Mirroring (how much the model's position is affected by the position the user takes), Attribution Bias (how much the model favors an idea attributed to the user), and Delusion Acceptance (how much the model plays along with delusional statements).

**Original Sycophancy Dataset**: A repository that includes datasets designed to evaluate sycophantic behavior of language models across varied free-form text-generation tasks from the paper "Towards Understanding Sycophancy in Language Models".

Based on the search results, here's what I found about HuggingFace datasets hallucination benchmarks leaderboards:

## Main Hallucination Leaderboards

There are several hallucination leaderboards available on Hugging Face:

1. **The Hallucinations Leaderboard**: A platform for evaluating the hallucination tendencies of LLMs, available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/hallucinations-leaderboard/leaderboard. The leaderboard uses a comprehensive set of benchmarks focusing on different aspects of hallucinations, such as factuality and faithfulness, across various tasks, including question-answering, summarisation, and reading comprehension.

2. **Vectara's Hallucination Leaderboard**: This leaderboard uses HHEM-2.3, Vectara's commercial hallucination evaluation model, to compute the LLM rankings.

3. **LLM Hallucination Detection Leaderboard**: This application displays a leaderboard comparing the hallucination rates of different language models in both RAG and non-RAG settings.

## Tasks and Datasets

The Hallucination Leaderboard includes a variety of tasks: Closed-book Open-domain QA (NQ Open, TriviaQA, TruthfulQA), fact-checking (FEVER), and hallucination detection (FaithDial, True-False, and HaluEval).

## Submission and Features

The leaderboard is available and you can submit models by clicking on Submit, with analytics functionalities being added in upcoming weeks.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about these datasets and concepts:

## PersonaChat Dataset

The Persona-Chat dataset, proposed by Zhang et al. (2018), has become a benchmark for persona-based conversation generation. In Persona-Chat conversations, each person is given their own persona at the beginning of the chat but does not know the persona of the other person.

## Sycophancy Datasets

Sycophancy evaluation datasets are designed to evaluate sycophantic behavior of language models across varied free-form text-generation tasks. Research has identified directions in language models' activation space—persona vectors—underlying several traits, such as evil, sycophancy, and propensity to hallucinate.

Anthropic's sycophancy datasets include two agree vs. disagree format datasets (Sycophancy on NLP survey, Sycophancy on PhilPapers 2020) and one A/B statement choice dataset (Sycophancy on political typology).

## Recent Developments

Researchers have proposed a pipeline for generating Open-Domain Dialogue data in multiple target languages using LLMs, with demonstrations applied to the PersonaChat dataset.

The search results show these are active research areas focused on understanding language model behavior and improving conversational AI through persona-based datasets and sycophancy evaluation.

Based on the search results, I found several relevant research efforts on confabulation detection in LLMs and epistemic failures:

## Key Datasets and Benchmarks

The ReFACT dataset is composed of expert-annotated non-factual information from answers originally provided in r/Askscience, specifically designed for scientific contexts. The dataset uses two strategies for creating confabulated counterparts: entity replacement (modifying key terms or entities) and negation (reversing the logical polarity of the statement).

Research evaluating confabulation detection has utilized HotpotQA and Natural Questions datasets, among other benchmarks used for question-answering tasks.

## Epistemic Failure and Confabulation

Most prior work on confabulation has focused on general patterns, with limited attention to domain-specific epistemic failures in scientific context. Confabulations are described as an error subset of fluent, coherent and contextually plausible content that nonetheless contains subtle factual inaccuracies.

Large Language Models are prone to generating fluent but incorrect content, known as confabulation, which poses increasing risks in multi-turn or agentic applications where outputs may be reused as context.

## Detection Methods

Entropy-based uncertainty estimators for LLMs have been proposed to detect confabulations—which are arbitrary and incorrect generations. Semantic entropy is computed over the meanings of sentences rather than their lexical or syntactic forms; and a high semantic entropy indicates high uncertainty in the generated content.

Experiments reveal that both open and closed commercial LLMs largely fail to detect, locate, and correct confabulation, highlighting ongoing challenges in this area.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings related to deception detection, LLM manipulation, and relevant datasets:

## Key Research Areas

### LLM-Generated Deception Detection

Research has collected benchmark corpora including authentic reviews (HAAR), human-authored fake reviews (HAFR), and LLM-based reviews (such as DeepSeek-authored reviews; DSAR). Supervised machine learning and deep learning models have been trained to distinguish fake and LLM-generated reviews from authentic ones.

### Multimodal Deception Detection

MM-DeceptionBench is the first benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate multimodal deception, covering six categories of strategic manipulation. A novel multi-agent debate framework with images compels models to ground their claims in visual evidence, substantially improving detectability.

### Datasets Used

Common datasets include real-life trial data from experiments at Michigan and the Deceptive Opinion Spam Corpus v1.4. Additionally, a real trial dataset enriched with emotional features has been constructed, with manual emotional annotation developing a dataset with ten refined emotional features.

### Behavioral Analysis of Deceptive LLMs

Datasets targeting categories of manipulative behavior (e.g., false credentials, emotional provocation) combined with intervention methods such as honesty-steered alternatives support enhanced detection. Research includes signaling games where deception rates are linked to reward structure, and open-ended interaction simulations that assess both deceptive intention and realized capability.

The field encompasses text-based deception, multimodal manipulation, psychological behavior analysis, and game-theoretic approaches to understanding and detecting LLM deception.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the FaithDial dataset:

## FaithDial: A Faithful Benchmark for Information-Seeking Dialogue

FaithDial is a benchmark for hallucination-free dialogues created by editing hallucinated responses in the Wizard of Wikipedia (WoW) benchmark.

### Dataset Overview

FaithDial contains around 50K turns across 5.5K conversations. A response was labeled as hallucination if it contained information not supported by the given knowledge.

### Purpose and Applications

The goal of information-seeking dialogue is to respond to seeker queries with natural language utterances that are grounded on knowledge sources. However, dialogue systems often produce unsupported utterances, a phenomenon known as hallucination.

FaithDial can serve as training signal for a hallucination critic, which discriminates whether an utterance is faithful or not, and boosts the performance by 12.8 F1 score on the BEGIN benchmark compared to existing datasets for dialogue coherence, as well as for high-quality dialogue generation.

### Key Results

The benefits of FaithDial generalize to zero-shot transfer on other datasets, such as CMU-Dog and TopicalChat. Human evaluation reveals that responses generated by models trained on FaithDial are perceived as more interpretable, cooperative, and engaging.

Here are the key results from the web search about semantic entropy for hallucination detection:

## Overview

Semantic entropy detects confabulations in free-form text generation across a range of language models and domains, without previous domain knowledge. Semantic entropy can detect hallucinations by estimating uncertainty in the space semantic meaning for a set of model generations.

## Core Concept

Semantic entropy clusters answers which share meanings before computing the entropy. A low semantic entropy shows that the LLM is confident about the meaning.

## Benchmark Datasets

Research on semantic entropy has been evaluated across multiple benchmark datasets:

Evaluations cover question answering in trivia knowledge (TriviaQA), general knowledge (SQuAD 1.1), life sciences (BioASQ) and open-domain natural questions (NQ-Open) derived from actual queries to Google Search. In addition, semantic entropy detects confabulations in mathematical word problems (SVAMP) and in a biography-generation dataset, FactualBio.

## Related Methods

Two major follow-up approaches have emerged:

1. **Semantic Entropy Probes (SEPs)**: SEPs directly approximate SE from the hidden states of a single generation. SEPs are simple to train and do not require sampling multiple model generations at test time, reducing the overhead of semantic uncertainty quantification to almost zero.

2. **Vision-Amplified Semantic Entropy (VASE)**: VASE incorporates weak image transformations and amplifies the impact of visual input to improve hallucination detection in medical VQA. VASE was evaluated on two VQA benchmarking datasets: MIMIC-Diff-VQA and VQA-RAD, with MIMIC-Diff-VQA being a chest X-ray VQA dataset with 700,703 question-answer pairs.

Based on the search results, here's information about these important question answering benchmark datasets:

## SQuAD (Stanford Question Answering Dataset)

SQuAD is a reading comprehension dataset, consisting of questions posed by crowdworkers on a set of Wikipedia articles, where the answer to every question is a segment of text, or span, from the corresponding reading passage, or the question might be unanswerable. The first version of the SQuAD dataset had 107,702 questions. The questions and answer were generated by crowdworkers. To this end, each crowdworker was presented with a Wikipedia passage and tasked with asking and answering up to five questions based on the content of the passage. SQuAD2.0 combines the 100,000 questions in SQuAD1.1 with over 50,000 unanswerable questions written adversarially by crowdworkers to look similar to answerable ones.

## HotpotQA

HotpotQA is a question answering dataset featuring natural, multi-hop questions, with strong supervision for supporting facts to enable more explainable question answering systems. HotpotQA contains 113k Wikipedia-based question-answer pairs with four key features: (1) the questions require finding and reasoning over multiple supporting documents to answer; (2) the questions are diverse and not constrained to any pre-existing knowledge bases or knowledge schemas; (3) sentence-level supporting facts are provided for reasoning, allowing QA systems to reason with strong supervision and explain the predictions; (4) a new type of factoid comparison questions to test QA systems' ability to extract relevant facts and perform necessary comparison.

## NaturalQuestions

NaturalQuestions, along with SQuAD and HotpotQA, contains questions and answers written by annotators who have first read a short text containing the answer. This dataset represents a shift toward more naturally occurring questions compared to some earlier datasets.

These three datasets have become central benchmarks for evaluating question answering systems, with each emphasizing different aspects of the task—SQuAD on span-based reading comprehension, HotpotQA on multi-hop reasoning with explanations, and NaturalQuestions on natural question formulation.

Based on the search results, here are the key hallucination detection benchmarks and leaderboards available:

## Major Leaderboards and Benchmarks

**Vectara's Hallucination Leaderboard**
This leaderboard uses HHEM-2.3, Vectara's commercial hallucination evaluation model, to compute LLM rankings. The updated leaderboard includes over 7,700 articles spanning diverse domains like law, medicine, finance, education and technology. The original hallucination leaderboard has tracked hallucination rates for LLMs since 2023 using their HHEM hallucination detection model.

**The Hallucinations Leaderboard (Hugging Face/Edinburgh)**
The Hallucinations Leaderboard evaluates LLMs on an array of hallucination-related benchmarks. The leaderboard includes tasks such as Closed-book Open-domain QA (NQ Open, TriviaQA) and TruthfulQA, and aims to evaluate a wide array of LLMs against benchmarks specifically designed to assess hallucination-related issues via in-context learning.

**HaluEval Benchmark**
HaluEval includes 5,000 general user queries with ChatGPT responses and 30,000 task-specific examples from three tasks: question answering, knowledge-grounded dialogue, and text summarization.

**Awesome Hallucination Detection**
The repository lists various benchmarks including MMHal-Bench and GAVIE for hallucination evaluation in LVLMs, and proposes frameworks like Pelican that detect and mitigate visual hallucinations through claim verification by decomposing claims into sub-claims and using program-of-thought to verify each sub-claim's truth.

## Recent Developments

Vectara recently introduced FaithJudge, an LLM-as-a-judge framework that leverages diverse human-annotated hallucination examples to improve hallucination evaluation, with an enhanced leaderboard that benchmarks LLMs on RAG faithfulness in summarization, question-answering, and data-to-text generation tasks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the ALCE dataset:

## ALCE: Attributable Language Model Outputs Dataset

ALCE is the first benchmark for Automatic LLMs' Citation Evaluation. The work aims to allow LLMs to generate text with citations, improving their factual correctness and verifiability.

### Dataset Composition

ALCE contains three datasets: ASQA, QAMPARI, and ELI5. ALCE collects a diverse set of questions and retrieval corpora and requires building end-to-end systems to retrieve supporting evidence and generate answers with citations.

### Evaluation Metrics

The benchmark develops automatic metrics along three dimensions -- fluency, correctness, and citation quality -- and demonstrates their strong correlation with human judgements. These automatic metrics correlate with human judgements.

### Key Findings

Experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs and novel prompting strategies show that current systems have considerable room for improvement—for example, on the ELI5 dataset, even the best models lack complete citation support 50% of the time.

### Availability

The code and data are available at https://github.com/princeton-nlp/ALCE.

## Related Research on Bias and Sycophancy

While Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is effective in aligning models with human preferences, studies have observed that it can simultaneously amplify sycophantic behavior.

Recent work in this area includes:

1. **Sycophancy Answer Assessment (SAA) Dataset**: Researchers have proposed assessing the user's expected answer rather than ignoring it, developing the Sycophancy Answer Assessment (SAA) dataset.

2. **Sycophancy Measurement**: Sycophancy is a general behavior of RLHF-trained conversational models, with models wrongly admitting mistakes, giving biased feedback, and mimicking user errors across Anthropic, OpenAI, and Meta assistants.

3. **Preference Model Bias**: Analysis of preference data found that "matching user beliefs and biases" was highly predictive of human judgments.

If you're looking for more specific information about the "BiPaR" dataset, you may want to try a more targeted search or check academic repositories like ArXiv or ACL Anthology directly.

Based on the search results, here are the instructions for downloading the HotpotQA dataset from Hugging Face:

## Official HotpotQA Dataset

The main HotpotQA dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/hotpotqa/hotpot_qa.

### Using the Datasets Library

The simplest way to download HotpotQA is using the Hugging Face `datasets` library. You can load the dataset using: `from datasets import load_dataset` and then `load_dataset()` will download the dataset and make a copy of the data in Hugging Face Dataset format.

### Dataset Details

HotpotQA is a dataset with 113k Wikipedia-based question-answer pairs with four key features: (1) the questions require finding and reasoning over multiple supporting documents to answer; (2) the questions are diverse and not constrained to any pre-existing knowledge bases or knowledge schemas; (3) sentence-level supporting facts are provided for reasoning; (4) it offers factoid comparison questions.

The dataset includes two configurations: "distractor" and "fullwiki", with training and validation splits containing 90,447 and 7,405 examples respectively for the distractor configuration.

### Alternative Variants

If you're interested in related versions, there are also HotpotQA variants available through other organizations on Hugging Face, such as BeIR, MTEB, and sentence-transformers versions, each with different preprocessing or use cases.

## MultiWOZ and Hallucination Detection

MultiWOZ 2.2 is a multi-domain, task-oriented dialogue dataset featuring over 10,000 dialogues with extensive annotation corrections and a redefined slot ontology.

### Hallucination Detection Methods

MultiWOZ 2.2 employs a mixture of automated detection, manual inspection, and crowd-sourcing to correct annotation errors, including hallucinated values that are detected via automated scans where values not grounded in prior dialog context were identified and flagged.

### Correction Statistics

The dataset corrected dialogue-state annotations in 17.3% of user turns, affecting 28.2% of dialogues. The dataset employs automated detection, manual inspection, and crowd-sourcing to standardize slot-span labels and correct errors like hallucinations and inconsistencies.

### Best Practices for Hallucination Mitigation

Recommended best practices include schema definition prior to annotation, enforcing controlled value selection (dropdowns for categorical, span marking for non-categorical), automated validation for non-ontology values or missing provenance, and crowd-filled correction pipelines, which are essential to mitigate annotation drift, slot-value hallucination, and overfitting to frequent entities.

The search results indicate that while MultiWOZ includes hallucination detection as part of its annotation correction process, there doesn't appear to be a dedicated separate dataset specifically called a "MultiWOZ dialogue hallucination detection dataset." Rather, hallucination detection is integrated into the MultiWOZ 2.2 annotation methodology.

### CATEGORY 2: SYCOPHANCY DETECTION DATASETS

#### 7. **SycEval: Evaluating LLM Sycophancy**
- **Paper**: https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.08177

- **Paper**: https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.13995

#### 9. **Syco-Bench: Multi-Part Sycophancy Benchmark**
- **Website**: https://www.syco-bench.com/

  - Closed-book QA: NQ Open, TriviaQA, TruthfulQA (MC1, MC2, generative)
  - Fact-checking: FEVER
  - Hallucination detection: FaithDial, True-False, HaluEval
- **Blog**: https://huggingface.co/blog/leaderboard-hallucinations

#### 26. **Vectara's Hallucination Leaderboard**
- **Website**: https://huggingface.co/spaces/vectara/leaderboard
- **Evaluation Model**: HHEM-2.3 hallucination detector
- **Coverage**: 7,700+ articles across law, medicine, finance, education, technology
- **GitHub**: https://github.com/vectara/hallucination-leaderboard

#### 27. **LLM Hallucination Detection Leaderboard (Kluster AI)**
- **Website**: https://huggingface.co/spaces/kluster-ai/LLM-Hallucination-Detection-Leaderboard
- **Focus**: RAG and non-RAG hallucination comparison

---

### CATEGORY 9: COMPREHENSIVE RESOURCES

#### 28. **Awesome Hallucination Detection (GitHub)**
- **Repository**: https://github.com/EdinburghNLP/awesome-hallucination-detection
- **Type**: Curated list of papers and benchmarks
- **Coverage**: Hallucination detection methods and datasets

#### 29. **Papers with Code: Hallucination Task**
- **URL**: https://paperswithcode.com/task/hallucination
- **Type**: Centralized leaderboard with state-of-the-art methods

---

## QUICK REFERENCE TABLE: DOWNLOAD INSTRUCTIONS

| Dataset | HuggingFace ID | Download Command | Alternative Source |
|---------|---|---|---|
| TruthfulQA | `truthful_qa` | `datasets.load_dataset('truthful_qa', 'generation')` | https://github.com/sylinrl/TruthfulQA |
| HaluEval | `flowaicom/HaluEval` | `datasets.load_dataset('flowaicom/HaluEval')` | https://github.com/RUCAIBox/HaluEval |
| FEVER | `fever/fever` | `datasets.load_dataset('fever', 'v1.0')` | https://fever.ai |
| FaithDial | `mcgill-nlp/FaithDial` | `datasets.load_dataset('mcgill-nlp/FaithDial')` | https://mcgill-nlp.github.io/FaithDial/ |
| SQuAD | `squad` or `squad_v2` | `datasets.load_dataset('squad')` | https://rajpurkar.github.io/SQuAD-explorer/ |
| HotpotQA | `hotpotqa/hotpot_qa` | `datasets.load_dataset('hotpotqa/hotpot_qa')` | https://hotpotqa.github.io/ |
| Natural Questions | `natural_questions` | `datasets.load_dataset('natural_questions')` | Google Research |
| ALCE | N/A (Composite) | See GitHub | https://github.com/princeton-nlp/ALCE |

---

## KEY CATEGORIZATION BY TYPE OF FALSE OUTPUT

**Epistemic Failure (Hallucination/Confabulation):**
- TruthfulQA, HaluEval, FEVER, FaithDial, ReFACT, Semantic Entropy benchmarks

**Strategic/Incentive-Driven (Sycophancy, Deception):**
- SycEval, ELEPHANT, Syco-Bench, MM-DeceptionBench

**Knowledge Grounding & Attribution:**
- HotpotQA, ALCE, MultiWOZ, NQ-Open

**Evaluation & Detection Methods:**
- The Hallucinations Leaderboard, Vectara Leaderboard, Semantic Entropy Probes

---

## RECOMMENDED STARTING POINTS

1. **For comprehensive hallucination evaluation**: Use **The Hallucinations Leaderboard** (combines multiple benchmarks)
2. **For distinguishing epistemic vs strategic**: Pair **TruthfulQA** (factuality) with **SycEval**/**Syco-Bench** (sycophancy)
3. **For dialogue-specific hallucinations**: **FaithDial** or **HalluDial**
4. **For attribution/verifiability**: **ALCE** benchmark (multi-aspect evaluation)
5. **For confabulation detection**: **ReFACT** (scientific context) or **Semantic Entropy** methods
6. **For multimodal deception**: **MM-DeceptionBench**

input.command:
source .venv/bin/activate && python3 << 'PYEOF'
import requests, os, time

papers = [
    # Sycophancy & strategic deception
    ("2310.13548_sycophancy_understanding.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.13548.pdf"),  # Towards Understanding Sycophancy
    ("2308.14752_simple_probes_llm.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.14752.pdf"),  # Representation Engineering / probing
    ("2312.04963_sleeper_agents.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2312.04963.pdf"),  # Sleeper Agents deceptive alignment
    ("2305.11747_halueval.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.11747.pdf"),  # HaluEval benchmark
    ("2310.01405_representation_engineering.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.01405.pdf"),  # Representation Engineering
    ("2309.15840_llm_knows_true.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2309.15840.pdf"),  # Do LLMs Know What They Know?
    ("2212.03827_discovering_latent_knowledge.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2212.03827.pdf"),  # CCS: Discovering Latent Knowledge
    ("2310.06824_geometry_of_truth.pdf", "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2310.06824.pdf"),  # Geometry of Truth
]

os.makedirs("papers", exist_ok=True)
for fname, url in papers:
    path = f"papers/{fname}"
    if os.path.exists(path):
        print(f"SKIP {fname}")
        continue
    try:
        r = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
        if r.status_code == 200 and len(r.content) > 10000:
            with open(path, 'wb') as f:
                f.write(r.content)
            print(f"OK   {fname} ({len(r.content)//1024}KB)")
        else:
            print(f"FAIL {fname} status={r.status_code} size={len(r.content)}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"ERR  {fname}: {e}")
    time.sleep(0.5)
PYEOF

content.text:
Here is a detailed summary of the paper based on all four chunks.

---

## Paper Summary: "The Internal State of an LLM Knows When It's Lying"

**Authors:** Amos Azaria (Ariel University, Israel) and Tom Mitchell (Carnegie Mellon University)
**Published:** arXiv:2304.13734v2, October 17, 2023

---

### 1. Research Question and Main Hypothesis

The central question is: **Can the internal hidden-layer activations of an LLM be used to determine whether a statement it reads or generates is true or false?**

The hypothesis is that LLMs must maintain some internal representation of truthfulness in order to generate coherent follow-up tokens. For example, after generating "The sun orbits the Earth," the LLM is more likely to follow up with a correction, implying it has some internal signal that the statement is false. The authors hypothesize this signal is extractable from hidden layer activations even though the LLM still produces the false statement in the first place.

They identify three reasons why an LLM can "know" a statement is false yet still generate it:
- Token-by-token commitment: greedy/local token choices can lead to globally false statements (the "Pluto is the smallest..." example).
- A single incorrect completion may have higher probability than any individual correct completion.
- Sampling from the probability distribution can yield low-probability (false) tokens.

---

### 2. Methodology (SAPLMA)

The method is called **SAPLMA** (Statement Accuracy Prediction based on Language Model Activations):

- **Input:** Hidden layer activation values from a specific layer of the LLM, captured at the final token position of a statement.
- **Classifier architecture:** A feedforward neural network with three hidden layers (256, 128, 64 units), ReLU activations, sigmoid output, trained with Adam optimizer for 5 epochs. No hyperparameter tuning.
- **Layer selection:** They test 5 different layers from the 32-layer models: last (32), 28th, 24th, 20th, and middle (16th). Each layer has 4096 hidden units.
- **Cross-topic evaluation:** Critically, the classifier is trained on 5 out of 6 topics and tested on the held-out 6th topic. This ensures the classifier learns a general "truthfulness" representation rather than topic-specific patterns.
- **LLMs used:** Facebook OPT-6.7b and LLAMA2-7b (both 32-layer, 7B-parameter models).
- Each classifier is trained 3 times with different random seeds; results are averaged.

---

### 3. Datasets

**A. True-False Dataset (custom, released by the authors):**
- 6,084 sentences across 6 topics:
  - Cities (1,458), Inventions (876), Chemical Elements (930), Animals (1,008), Companies (1,200), Scientific Facts (612)
- For the first 5 topics: true statements were composed from reliable source tables (e.g., element name + correct atomic number); false statements were created by swapping a property value with one from a different row.
- For Scientific Facts: ChatGPT (Feb 13 version) generated well-known facts and their negations; these were manually curated and verified by 2 human annotators (48 ambiguous facts removed).

**B. LLM-Generated Statements Dataset:**
- 245 statements generated by OPT-6.7b itself (given a true seed statement, the LLM generated a following statement using greedy decoding).
- Non-factual statements filtered out. Remaining statements fact-checked by 3 human judges (97.82% agreement, Cohen's Kappa = 0.9566).
- 48.6% labeled true -- roughly balanced.

**Dataset availability:** The true-false dataset is released at `azariaa.com/Content/Datasets/true-false-dataset.zip`.

---

### 4. Key Results

**On the True-False Dataset (externally provided statements):**

| Model | OPT-6.7b Best (20th layer) Avg | LLAMA2-7b Best (16th layer) Avg |
|-------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| SAPLMA | 71.0% | **83.0%** |
| BERT baseline | 54.3% | -- |
| 3-shot prompting | 53.7% | -- |
| 5-shot prompting | 53.7% | -- |
| "It-is-true" probability | 55.9% | -- |

- SAPLMA with OPT-6.7b: best layer is 20th, average accuracy 71.0%. Per-topic range: ~58% (elements) to ~81% (cities).
- SAPLMA with LLAMA2-7b: best layer is 16th (middle), average accuracy 83.0%. Per-topic range: ~69% (elements) to ~92% (cities).
- Zero-shot prompting completely failed (no better than 52%).
- Training accuracy was 86.4%, and a randomized-label control achieved only 62.5%, confirming the classifier exploits genuine structure.

**On LLM-Generated Statements (OPT-6.7b):**
- With default 0.5 threshold: best accuracy 63.6% (28th layer), AUC 0.76.
- With calibrated threshold (using 30% validation split): best accuracy **71.3%** (28th layer).
- BERT and few-shot baselines performed near chance (~50-51%).
- GPT-4, when prompted to classify the same 245 statements, achieved 84.4% accuracy.

**SAPLMA vs. raw sentence probability (Table 5 qualitative analysis):**
- Sentence probabilities are heavily confounded by sentence length and word frequency (e.g., "The Earth is flat" has high probability because it appears often in training data).
- SAPLMA correctly handles these cases, assigning low truthfulness to "The Earth is flat" despite its high token probability.

---

### 5. Types of False Statements Considered

The paper considers:
- **Factually incorrect statements** with swapped properties (e.g., wrong atomic number, wrong city location)
- **Negations of scientific facts** (e.g., "Ice sinks in water due to its higher density")
- **LLM-generated false statements** arising from greedy token-by-token generation (hallucinations in the colloquial sense)
- **Common misconceptions** that may appear frequently in training data (e.g., "The Earth is flat")
- **Implicit truths** rarely stated explicitly (e.g., "Jennifer Aniston is a female person")

---

### 6. Distinction Between Types of Falsehood (Hallucination vs. Strategic Deception)

**This is a significant gap in the paper for your literature review.** The authors **do not distinguish between different types of falsehood**. They explicitly note in the Related Work section that their notion of "false" differs from the hallucination literature: most hallucination work defines it relative to a source passage (e.g., a summary containing information not in the article), whereas this paper focuses on absolute factual veracity. However, they do not taxonomize falsehoods into categories such as:

- Hallucination (the LLM lacks knowledge and confabulates)
- Strategic/intentional deception (the LLM "knows" the truth but outputs something different)
- Errors from token commitment (knows retrospectively but was trapped by greedy decoding)

The paper's title provocatively uses "lying," but the authors themselves acknowledge they are really detecting whether the LLM's internal state correlates with factual accuracy. They do not attempt to determine the *mechanism* or *intent* behind the falsehood. The three reasons they give for why LLMs produce false statements (token commitment, probability distribution shape, sampling) are all presented as mechanical/unintentional -- none involve strategic deception. The paper treats all false statements uniformly as a binary classification problem.

**This is a key limitation for research on distinguishing lie types.**

---

### 7. Limitations and Open Questions

The authors explicitly state the following limitations:

1. **Binary classification only:** The method classifies true vs. false but does not capture uncertainty/confidence gradations. In practice, detecting "unsure" would be more useful than binary true/false.
2. **English only:** Not tested on other languages, though they hypothesize multilingual transfer may work.
3. **Single-statement isolation:** Activations were collected for individual statements in isolation. In practice, LLMs generate multi-sentence responses where activation values accumulate context from both correct and incorrect preceding statements. They suggest a "discrete derivative" approach (subtracting previous activations) but have not tested it.
4. **Relatively small models:** Only tested on OPT-6.7b and LLAMA2-7b (both ~7B parameters). They intend to scale to larger LLMs.
5. **Optimal layer is model-dependent:** The best layer for OPT-6.7b (20th) differs from LLAMA2-7b (16th), and differs again for generated vs. externally provided statements. No principled method for layer selection is provided.
6. **Bias inheritance:** If the LLM has biases (e.g., about ethnic groups), SAPLMA may inherit and reproduce those biases in its truth classifications.
7. **No human interaction study:** Future work plans to test whether SAPLMA-augmented LLMs actually improve human trust and accuracy.
8. **Only uses final-token activations:** They plan to explore using activations from all tokens in the statement.

---

### 8. Code/Data Availability

- **Dataset:** The true-false dataset is publicly available at `azariaa.com/Content/Datasets/true-false-dataset.zip`.
- **Code:** No mention of released code or a GitHub repository. The classifier architecture and training procedure are described in enough detail to reproduce, but no code link is provided.

---

### Relevance to Your Literature Review on Detecting Different Types of Lies

This paper is foundational for probe-based truthfulness detection but has a critical gap for your research agenda: **it does not distinguish between types of falsehood**. All false statements -- whether from knowledge gaps, token-commitment artifacts, or any other source -- are treated identically. The paper provides strong evidence that LLM internal states encode *something* about truthfulness, but leaves entirely open the question of whether different *mechanisms* of falsehood (hallucination vs. strategic deception vs. knowledge gaps) produce distinguishable internal representations. This is a direct opening that your research could address.

content.text:
Here is a detailed summary of the paper based on all four chunks.

---

## Paper Summary: "The Internal State of an LLM Knows When It's Lying"

**Authors:** Amos Azaria (Ariel University, Israel) and Tom Mitchell (Carnegie Mellon University)
**Published:** arXiv:2304.13734v2, October 17, 2023

---

### 1. Research Question and Main Hypothesis

The central question is: **Can the internal hidden-layer activations of an LLM be used to determine whether a statement it reads or generates is true or false?**

The hypothesis is that LLMs must maintain some internal representation of truthfulness in order to generate coherent follow-up tokens. For example, after generating "The sun orbits the Earth," the LLM is more likely to follow up with a correction, implying it has some internal signal that the statement is false. The authors hypothesize this signal is extractable from hidden layer activations even though the LLM still produces the false statement in the first place.

They identify three reasons why an LLM can "know" a statement is false yet still generate it:
- Token-by-token commitment: greedy/local token choices can lead to globally false statements (the "Pluto is the smallest..." example).
- A single incorrect completion may have higher probability than any individual correct completion.
- Sampling from the probability distribution can yield low-probability (false) tokens.

---

### 2. Methodology (SAPLMA)

The method is called **SAPLMA** (Statement Accuracy Prediction based on Language Model Activations):

- **Input:** Hidden layer activation values from a specific layer of the LLM, captured at the final token position of a statement.
- **Classifier architecture:** A feedforward neural network with three hidden layers (256, 128, 64 units), ReLU activations, sigmoid output, trained with Adam optimizer for 5 epochs. No hyperparameter tuning.
- **Layer selection:** They test 5 different layers from the 32-layer models: last (32), 28th, 24th, 20th, and middle (16th). Each layer has 4096 hidden units.
- **Cross-topic evaluation:** Critically, the classifier is trained on 5 out of 6 topics and tested on the held-out 6th topic. This ensures the classifier learns a general "truthfulness" representation rather than topic-specific patterns.
- **LLMs used:** Facebook OPT-6.7b and LLAMA2-7b (both 32-layer, 7B-parameter models).
- Each classifier is trained 3 times with different random seeds; results are averaged.

---

### 3. Datasets

**A. True-False Dataset (custom, released by the authors):**
- 6,084 sentences across 6 topics:
  - Cities (1,458), Inventions (876), Chemical Elements (930), Animals (1,008), Companies (1,200), Scientific Facts (612)
- For the first 5 topics: true statements were composed from reliable source tables (e.g., element name + correct atomic number); false statements were created by swapping a property value with one from a different row.
- For Scientific Facts: ChatGPT (Feb 13 version) generated well-known facts and their negations; these were manually curated and verified by 2 human annotators (48 ambiguous facts removed).

**B. LLM-Generated Statements Dataset:**
- 245 statements generated by OPT-6.7b itself (given a true seed statement, the LLM generated a following statement using greedy decoding).
- Non-factual statements filtered out. Remaining statements fact-checked by 3 human judges (97.82% agreement, Cohen's Kappa = 0.9566).
- 48.6% labeled true -- roughly balanced.

**Dataset availability:** The true-false dataset is released at `azariaa.com/Content/Datasets/true-false-dataset.zip`.

---

### 4. Key Results

**On the True-False Dataset (externally provided statements):**

| Model | OPT-6.7b Best (20th layer) Avg | LLAMA2-7b Best (16th layer) Avg |
|-------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| SAPLMA | 71.0% | **83.0%** |
| BERT baseline | 54.3% | -- |
| 3-shot prompting | 53.7% | -- |
| 5-shot prompting | 53.7% | -- |
| "It-is-true" probability | 55.9% | -- |

- SAPLMA with OPT-6.7b: best layer is 20th, average accuracy 71.0%. Per-topic range: ~58% (elements) to ~81% (cities).
- SAPLMA with LLAMA2-7b: best layer is 16th (middle), average accuracy 83.0%. Per-topic range: ~69% (elements) to ~92% (cities).
- Zero-shot prompting completely failed (no better than 52%).
- Training accuracy was 86.4%, and a randomized-label control achieved only 62.5%, confirming the classifier exploits genuine structure.

**On LLM-Generated Statements (OPT-6.7b):**
- With default 0.5 threshold: best accuracy 63.6% (28th layer), AUC 0.76.
- With calibrated threshold (using 30% validation split): best accuracy **71.3%** (28th layer).
- BERT and few-shot baselines performed near chance (~50-51%).
- GPT-4, when prompted to classify the same 245 statements, achieved 84.4% accuracy.

**SAPLMA vs. raw sentence probability (Table 5 qualitative analysis):**
- Sentence probabilities are heavily confounded by sentence length and word frequency (e.g., "The Earth is flat" has high probability because it appears often in training data).
- SAPLMA correctly handles these cases, assigning low truthfulness to "The Earth is flat" despite its high token probability.

---

### 5. Types of False Statements Considered

The paper considers:
- **Factually incorrect statements** with swapped properties (e.g., wrong atomic number, wrong city location)
- **Negations of scientific facts** (e.g., "Ice sinks in water due to its higher density")
- **LLM-generated false statements** arising from greedy token-by-token generation (hallucinations in the colloquial sense)
- **Common misconceptions** that may appear frequently in training data (e.g., "The Earth is flat")
- **Implicit truths** rarely stated explicitly (e.g., "Jennifer Aniston is a female person")

---

### 6. Distinction Between Types of Falsehood (Hallucination vs. Strategic Deception)

**This is a significant gap in the paper for your literature review.** The authors **do not distinguish between different types of falsehood**. They explicitly note in the Related Work section that their notion of "false" differs from the hallucination literature: most hallucination work defines it relative to a source passage (e.g., a summary containing information not in the article), whereas this paper focuses on absolute factual veracity. However, they do not taxonomize falsehoods into categories such as:

- Hallucination (the LLM lacks knowledge and confabulates)
- Strategic/intentional deception (the LLM "knows" the truth but outputs something different)
- Errors from token commitment (knows retrospectively but was trapped by greedy decoding)

The paper's title provocatively uses "lying," but the authors themselves acknowledge they are really detecting whether the LLM's internal state correlates with factual accuracy. They do not attempt to determine the *mechanism* or *intent* behind the falsehood. The three reasons they give for why LLMs produce false statements (token commitment, probability distribution shape, sampling) are all presented as mechanical/unintentional -- none involve strategic deception. The paper treats all false statements uniformly as a binary classification problem.

**This is a key limitation for research on distinguishing lie types.**

---

### 7. Limitations and Open Questions

The authors explicitly state the following limitations:

1. **Binary classification only:** The method classifies true vs. false but does not capture uncertainty/confidence gradations. In practice, detecting "unsure" would be more useful than binary true/false.
2. **English only:** Not tested on other languages, though they hypothesize multilingual transfer may work.
3. **Single-statement isolation:** Activations were collected for individual statements in isolation. In practice, LLMs generate multi-sentence responses where activation values accumulate context from both correct and incorrect preceding statements. They suggest a "discrete derivative" approach (subtracting previous activations) but have not tested it.
4. **Relatively small models:** Only tested on OPT-6.7b and LLAMA2-7b (both ~7B parameters). They intend to scale to larger LLMs.
5. **Optimal layer is model-dependent:** The best layer for OPT-6.7b (20th) differs from LLAMA2-7b (16th), and differs again for generated vs. externally provided statements. No principled method for layer selection is provided.
6. **Bias inheritance:** If the LLM has biases (e.g., about ethnic groups), SAPLMA may inherit and reproduce those biases in its truth classifications.
7. **No human interaction study:** Future work plans to test whether SAPLMA-augmented LLMs actually improve human trust and accuracy.
8. **Only uses final-token activations:** They plan to explore using activations from all tokens in the statement.

---

### 8. Code/Data Availability

- **Dataset:** The true-false dataset is publicly available at `azariaa.com/Content/Datasets/true-false-dataset.zip`.
- **Code:** No mention of released code or a GitHub repository. The classifier architecture and training procedure are described in enough detail to reproduce, but no code link is provided.

---

### Relevance to Your Literature Review on Detecting Different Types of Lies

This paper is foundational for probe-based truthfulness detection but has a critical gap for your research agenda: **it does not distinguish between types of falsehood**. All false statements -- whether from knowledge gaps, token-commitment artifacts, or any other source -- are treated identically. The paper provides strong evidence that LLM internal states encode *something* about truthfulness, but leaves entirely open the question of whether different *mechanisms* of falsehood (hallucination vs. strategic deception vs. knowledge gaps) produce distinguishable internal representations. This is a direct opening that your research could address.

content.content:
=== PAGE 1 ===
Excitations of a two-dimensional supersolid
E. Poli,1 D. Baillie,2, 3 F. Ferlaino,1, 4 and P. B. Blakie2, 3
1Universität Innsbruck, Fakultät für Mathematik, Informatik und Physik,
Institut für Experimentalphysik, 6020 Innsbruck, Austria
2Dodd-Walls Centre for Photonic and Quantum Technologies, Dunedin 9054, New Zealand
3Department of Physics, University of Otago, Dunedin 9016, New Zealand
4Institut für Quantenoptik und Quanteninformation, Österreichische
Akademie der Wissenschaften, Technikerstraße 21a, 6020 Innsbruck, Austria
(Dated: December 13, 2024)
We present a theoretical study of the excitations of the two-dimensional supersolid state of a Bose-Einstein
condensate with either dipole-dipole interactions or soft-core interactions. This supersolid state has three gapless
excitation branches arising from the spontaneously broken continuous symmetries. Two of these branches are
related to longitudinal sound waves, similar to those in one-dimensional supersolids. The third branch is a
transverse wave arising from the non-zero shear modulus of the two-dimensional crystal. We present the results
of numerical calculations for the excitations and dynamic structure factor characterising the density fluctuations,
and study their behavior across the discontinuous superfluid to supersolid transition. We show that the speeds
of sound are described by a hydrodynamic theory that incorporates generalized elastic parameters, including the
shear modulus. Furthermore, we establish that dipolar and soft-core supersolids manifest distinct characteristics,
falling into the bulk incompressible and rigid lattice limits, respectively.
I.
INTRODUCTION
A supersolid is a state of matter in which phase and trans-
lational symmetries are broken.
Here we refer to a D-
dimensional supersolid as being a system that spontaneously
develops a D-dimensional crystal structure whilst still exhibit-
ing superfluidity. For this system it is expected that the bro-
ken symmetries will lead to (D+1)-Nambu-Goldstone modes
that manifest as gapless excitation branches [1]. The experi-
mental production of supersolid states in atomic gases [2–6]
has generated interest in their properties, including their ex-
citation spectra. The D = 1 case [7–13] has two gapless
excitation branches of longitudinal character, which are re-
ferred to as first and second sound1. A D = 2 supersolid has
recently been been produced using a dipolar Bose gas in an
oblate shaped trapping potential [16, 17]. This system has an
rich phase diagram with different ground state crystal patterns
separated by first order transitions [18–22], and is an interest-
ing system for considering the interplay of crystalline order
with vortices [23–26].
Here we study the excitation spectrum of a D = 2 super-
solid. We consider the case of a zero-temperature gas in the
thermodynamic limit, which admits a well-defined band struc-
ture allowing us to investigate the gapless modes in detail. We
show that a hydrodynamic theory provide

=== PAGE 2 ===
2
of the BdG equations in Sec. III. In Sec. IV we outline the
hydrodynamic theory for the D = 2 supersolid. We discuss
how the relevant elastic parameters that appear in this theory
can be determined from ground state calculations. The hydro-
dynamic predictions for the speeds of sound are compared to
those obtained from the BdG calculations. We then discuss
the key parameters distinguishing the behavior of the dipolar
and soft-core systems and identify the two relevant limits of
the hydrodynamic results to describe these systems. Finally,
we conclude in Sec. V.
II.
SUPERSOLID SYSTEMS
Here we introduce the two systems examined in this work.
